lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market firms providing recurring nonmedical weight-management services to external consumers, including structured behavioral programs, nutrition education, coaching, monitoring, group support, workshops, and digital programs when the firm remains accountable for service delivery.
lens.excluded_activities: Physician practices, medical nutrition therapy, prescription and telehealth clinics, surgical weight reduction, pharmacies, drug manufacturers, fitness-only facilities, meal or supplement retailers without a recurring service, self-service software without an accountable service operator, captive units, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Consumers commonly pay recurring subscriptions, program fees, workshop charges, or bundled coaching fees for guidance, tracking, education, and support. Some providers add food, supplement, or digital-product revenue, but the eligible model requires a repeat service relationship rather than product sales alone; medical prescription revenue is outside the code.

a.bridge: BLS describes dietitian and nutritionist work rather than the occupation mix or wage-weighted tasks of nonmedical 812191 firms. Assessment, meal-plan development, progress documentation, educational-material creation, research, scheduling, records, and routine follow-up are technically AI-addressable; counseling, motivation, relationship building, group facilitation, sensitive escalation, and accountable personalization are less substitutable. I apply that task pattern to coaches, counselors, and administrative staff in nonmedical centers, with a range for provider models that differ in live-service intensity. The proxy may overstate exposure where human coaching dominates and understate it for digital-first programs.
a.rationale: A large portion of information gathering, plan drafting, content, tracking, and routine communication can be assisted or automated, while trust, motivation, behavioral accountability, and judgment about when to refer a client remain human-intensive.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit occupational staffing matrix and role-level time study for nonmedical weight centers, separating live counseling and facilitation from plan preparation, tracking, messaging, marketing, and administration, would materially change this value.
caveats: Dietitians and nutritionists work across healthcare and institutional settings and are not a measured occupation mix for 812191.
The official code is nonmedical, so clinical duties described by BLS are excluded from the bridge.
The frozen compensation ratio combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts and may omit contractor or platform labor.

rho.bridge: The Census AI study measures adoption across U.S. firms and functions, not the implemented share of exposed work in weight programs. Its leading use cases in writing, document analysis, information search, sales, and marketing align with program content, follow-up, intake, and customer acquisition, but most adopting firms use AI in only a few functions and employment reductions are rare. I infer moderate five-year implementation, discounted for health-related accuracy, privacy, model monitoring, fragmented customer data, brand risk, and the need to preserve human support. The national proxy may overstate small-center capabilities but understate digital subscription platforms.
rho.rationale: The workflows are digitally representable and can support personalization at scale, yet production use must handle sensitive data, inconsistent self-reporting, behavioral safety, escalation, and customer expectations for credible human support.
rho.change_evidence: Provider-level longitudinal evidence on deployed AI workflows, quality incidents, human-review requirements, customer retention, and actual avoided labor hours across nonmedical weight programs would materially change this value.
caveats: The Census study is economy-wide and does not isolate weight-management firms.
Reported AI adoption does not measure durable workflow implementation or labor-hour reduction.
Medical compliance is excluded, but consumer privacy and safety constraints still matter.

e.bridge: The Census definition directly describes nonmedical services that assist clients in attaining or maintaining a desired weight, which normally implies an external customer and repeat program relationship. It does not measure eligibility among the frozen EBITDA-band firms. I apply a high share while allowing for product-heavy operators, medical miscoding, software-only products, franchise or licensing entities without accountable delivery, and normalized economics that do not fit the estimate. The result may overstate eligibility if the generic margin bridge captures firms whose revenue is principally product sales or clinical services.
e.rationale: Correctly classified operating centers generally provide ongoing consumer services, so most genuine firms already inside the band should satisfy the external recurring-service lens after excluding medical and product-only models.
e.change_evidence: A named census of the estimated EBITDA-band firms with service-versus-product revenue, medical licensure, recurring customer contracts, ownership structure, and normalized EBITDA would materially change this value.
caveats: The frozen count of 59 is derived with a generic 15.65% margin rather than verified from named firms.
Large branded providers can combine behavioral, digital, clinical, franchise, licensing, and product revenue that span classification boundaries.
Eligibility depends on the operating entity, not merely the consumer brand.

s5.bridge: Gallup reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, but it covers all industries and intentions rather than completed qualifying transfers. I discount for failed processes, closures, franchise transfers, internal reorganizations, and the possibility that challenged centers lose transferable customers or staff before sale. Industry disruption may increase forced processes while reducing buyer appetite, so the error direction is ambiguous and the range remains wide.
s5.rationale: Employer firms are more transferable than solo practices, but brand dependence, customer churn, shifting treatment preferences, and digital competition can impair continuity under new control.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of completed and failed sale processes for nonmedical weight-management providers in the fixed EBITDA band, including customer-retention outcomes after control transfer, would materially change this probability.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry and measures plans rather than completed control transfers.
The public-company example is not an LMM transfer observation and its financial reorganization is not itself a qualifying transfer.
No representative private transaction series for 812191 was found.

q.bridge: WeightWatchers is a large branded company spanning behavioral and clinical offerings, not a representative 812191 LMM firm, but its disclosed subscription model, subscriber changes, ARPU movement, discounts, and marketing intensity illustrate the commercial mechanics. Subscription and program fees have no automatic cost pass-through, supporting initial retention, while easy cancellation, promotional pricing, low switching costs, digital competitors, and required marketing push benefits toward customers. The proxy may understate capture for differentiated local coaching and overstate it for commoditized digital programs.
q.rationale: Fixed subscription and bundled-program pricing lets an operator retain near-term productivity gains, but renewals expose savings to price competition and customers may demand lower prices, better personalization, or more support rather than accepting unchanged service.
q.change_evidence: Cohort-level data from LMM nonmedical providers linking AI deployment to service cost, subscription price, discounts, churn, paid acquisition, engagement, and contribution margin over five years would materially change this value.
caveats: WeightWatchers is much larger than the fixed band and includes medical services outside 812191.
Its reported changes reflect brand, restructuring, marketing, and GLP-1 effects rather than isolated AI economics.
No source directly measures retained AI benefit in this industry.

d5.bridge: CDC measures a large persistent population need, BLS projects dietitian and nutritionist employment to grow 6% from 2024 to 2034, KFF documents meaningful GLP-1 use, and WeightWatchers reported a 17% year-over-year decline in Q4 2025 behavioral revenue alongside growth in clinical subscriptions. I translate these mixed signals into slightly declining base-case five-year demand for the current nonmedical service basket, with a wide range for behavioral support becoming either displaced by medication and self-service apps or complementary to long-term weight management. The public-company decline may overstate broad industry pressure, while occupation growth may overstate nonmedical center demand.
d5.rationale: Obesity and overweight prevalence sustain the underlying need, but current nonmedical programs face structural substitution from prescription treatment, digital self-service, and integrated clinical platforms. Human behavioral support can remain complementary, especially for adherence and weight maintenance.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year cohort of inflation-adjusted nonmedical program revenue, active members, visit or coaching volume, and retention segmented by GLP-1 use and delivery model would materially change this multiplier.
caveats: Obesity prevalence measures need, not paid demand for commercial nonmedical programs.
Dietitian employment spans healthcare and institutional settings outside this code.
WeightWatchers is a single large provider with clinical operations and a recent financial reorganization.
GLP-1 adoption can substitute for, complement, or increase engagement with behavioral services.

o.bridge: BLS duties show that assessment, counseling, plan adjustment, education, and progress monitoring combine structured information work with interpersonal judgment. WeightWatchers demonstrates that weight-management services can already be delivered as digital subscriptions, while its strategic emphasis on integrated support and community indicates continuing value from accountable service. I assign a majority of year-five quantity to operator-required coaching, escalation, motivation, and program accountability, while allowing software, self-service, and medication-centered pathways to eliminate a material share. The large-brand proxy may overstate digital substitutability for local high-touch centers.
o.rationale: Routine information and tracking can move to software, but sustained behavior change, sensitive conversations, motivation, group facilitation, exception handling, and responsibility for an appropriate nonmedical program continue to require a service operator for many customers.
o.change_evidence: Randomized or matched customer cohorts comparing fully self-service software with human-supported nonmedical programs on retention, adherence, safety escalation, and durable outcomes would materially change this value.
caveats: Clinical diagnosis, prescribing, and medical monitoring are outside the lens.
The share depends strongly on whether customers value community and accountability or primarily want information and tracking.
Medication effects belong in service demand and operator need, not AI implementation.

narrative.executive_view: Diet and weight reducing centers fit the recurring external-service lens when they provide nonmedical behavioral programs, coaching, education, and monitoring. Their information-heavy work offers meaningful AI assistance, but digital self-service and GLP-1-centered clinical alternatives also threaten the current service basket, and the frozen 59-firm population is a generic margin-bridge estimate rather than a verified target list.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can draft personalized meal and behavior plans, summarize intake, generate education, track progress, segment members, automate routine outreach, support coaches, and improve marketing. Human value remains in motivation, empathy, group facilitation, exception handling, relationship continuity, and deciding when a nonmedical client should be referred for professional care.
narrative.value_capture: Subscription and bundled-program pricing can retain early productivity benefits because there is no direct cost pass-through. Retention erodes at renewal through discounts, churn, easy comparison with apps, marketing reinvestment, and pressure to add better personalization or human support.
narrative.firm_availability: Employer businesses can transfer, but no industry-specific LMM transaction series was found. Brand dependence, member churn, franchise rights, product or clinical revenue, and staff continuity complicate transferability, while the frozen count depends on a generic margin assumption.
narrative.demand_durability: The underlying population need is large and persistent, but paid nonmedical programs are not protected from substitution. Recent behavioral subscription weakness at a major provider and rising GLP-1 use point downward, while nutrition-service growth and the role of coaching in adherence and maintenance provide an offset.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The industry boundary is a central risk: medical clinics belong outside 812191 even when consumers see one weight-management brand. Other uncertainties include rapid treatment change, privacy and accuracy obligations, customer churn, digital-platform competition, product-heavy revenue, a mixed contractor workforce, and lack of direct evidence on LMM AI economics or control transfers.
narrative.principal_driver: Structured, recurring coaching and tracking workflows create substantial AI-assistance potential while a persistent population need supports continued demand for effective programs.
narrative.principal_weakness: The current nonmedical service basket faces simultaneous substitution from self-service software, integrated clinical platforms, and GLP-1 medications.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 812191 Diet and Weight Reducing Centers
sources.supports: Official classification of establishments providing nonmedical services to help clients attain or maintain a desired weight and the exclusion of medical weight-reduction services.

sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Dietitians and Nutritionists
sources.supports: Proxy task content including assessment, counseling, plan development, monitoring, education, documentation, marketing and administration, plus the 2024-to-2034 employment projection.

sources.title: The Microstructure of AI Diffusion: Evidence from Firms, Business Functions, and Worker Tasks
sources.supports: National firm AI adoption, functional concentration, leading task uses, augmentation patterns, and limited observed employment reductions.

sources.title: Prevalence of Overweight, Obesity, and Severe Obesity Among Adults Age 20 and Older
sources.supports: Measured U.S. adult obesity, severe-obesity, and overweight prevalence for August 2021 through August 2023.

sources.title: KFF Health Tracking Poll May 2024: The Public's Use and Views of GLP-1 Drugs
sources.supports: Reported adult GLP-1 awareness and use, including use among people told they were overweight or had obesity, and affordability constraints.

sources.title: WeightWatchers Q4 2025 Shareholder Letter
sources.supports: Company-specific proxy for behavioral and clinical subscription trends, subscriber changes, ARPU, marketing, integrated-care strategy, and GLP-1-related structural pressure.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Five-year sale or ownership-transfer intentions among employer-business owners, used as a cross-industry transfer proxy.
